speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 4, 1996. The survivor is Lili Meier. And this is tape four. You found this album, and then you took it with you. And you were going back to Bilky. Tell me about that. Why did you decide to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would have never forgive myself if I wouldn't have gone and look and wait. I was there--

speaker: interviewer
text: For what?

speaker: interviewee
text: For if somebody is alive, they want to-- if they would return. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Unfortunately, no one did.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Bilky, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going back to watch our house. And somebody told me that somebody is living in the house. And I said, not for long. So I went to the city of Bilky there. And I said, I want the house back.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very, very nice to me because they knew me. Who else came back? I says, I don't know. But I'm here, and I want the house back. So they send right away some people to clean up the house, and they told me that I can get-- go into it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people's houses, survivors, the houses weren't spared. They just demolished them. But our house was-- and the furniture was missing. But I-- at least I was able to live at it-- live in there for a while. And then one day I went to the train. I-- Max was the only one who got out of that train in Bilky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Max who?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zelmanovic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met him at the train station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you known him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We grew up together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after you met him at the train station did you marry him?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a month later.

speaker: interviewer
text: A month. And how long--

speaker: interviewee
text: He want-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wanted us to stay there in Bilky. And I says, nuh-uh. He says, look. I have a business, and you wouldn't have to worry. I says, I want to get out of here. I say, I'll still leave it at my neighbors where I could be reached. And if somebody survives, they will notify them, and they'll-- and they'll find out where I am, and-- but unfortunately it didn't happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of his family survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: A brother of his was in the Czechoslovakian Army. And they marched him to Russia. And he passed away about three years ago. They lived in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you left Bilky?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a city where he had a restaurant, Max. And he says, look. This is all mine. You don't have to worry. You don't have to go to America. I says, I want to go to America. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uzhorod.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was also in the Carpathian Mountain.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I said, either you stay [LAUGHS] or you come. He says, I'm coming. [LAUGHS] But we already had to go under the wires, and we had people who helped us. We paid them to take us to this particular city, and so we can go on the train. Money we didn't have, but they said-- they also said that we could bribe the-- the people on the train and-- and give them liquor. So as long as you had enough liquor to give them, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where were you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going to the Sudeten. Because his brother was already there. He inherited a big farm. But that was no place for me. I was never a farm girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went and we stayed with them, because there was a big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mansion they had there. And I start traveling to Prague. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But unfortunately, we had to have identification, who we are.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met a friend in Prague by accident in the street. She says, guess where I just came from? I says, tell me. I'm not gonna guess. I don't know how to guess. And she told me that she went to this-- he was a teacher, but-- where-- what is the person called in school? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Not a teacher, but on the higher level? Principal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Principal. I'm sorry. And I said, so what did you do there? She said, well, didn't you know we have to identify ourselves? And I heard-- I found out where he is living, and I went there. I just came from there. And he gave me everything notarized that he knows me, he-- all his life. And I say, I guess-- I guess I'll have to do the same. So I called Max from Prague. I says, honey, I'm not coming. I'm here. I was already five months pregnant with Esther. He say, better take care of yourself and of that baby, America or no America. I says, don't worry. I'll be all right. So sure enough, I got on the train, and I came to the teacher. I had the address and everything. He was so happy to see me. I says, but-- all you remember is a little girl, a little 9, 10-year-old girl. I says, and here I am. He says, you were always a good student. I was-- we were always proud of you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Had it not been for the album, I wouldn't have been here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- we needed to-- to identify ourselves, and we didn't have but one. But being-- he gave me a notarized paper, the principal, and was easier already for me, a little bit, to get established.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our passports would expire every six months. And we didn't have the money for it. It was like equivalent to $50 to renew a new passport. Every six months, it would be-- expire. And so one day, I decided to-- to go into the city. And I-- there was-- somebody said that there was a man who is helping the refugees. I says, with money? And he says, yeah. So I-- I found out that he's a rabbi. And I went to him. And I says, could you help us out? I say, we are planning to go to America, but we need money to renew our passport again. He says, well, we don't have money to help you out. I says, wait a minute. I have something. Maybe that will take us to America. I went home by bus. I came back with the album. I says, here I am. I says, do you know anybody that could help us out? Would you sell the album?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, no. I'm not selling it. I want to keep that. I have family in there. That's-- those are the only pictures I have. So he picked up the phone, and he calls Prague, the Jewish Museum. And he says, look. I have somebody here with something that I've never seen. And she needs like $50 to renew her passport. Well, we'll-- we are sending you the money right away if it's what you say it is. It's a-- an album from-- from Auschwitz. So sure enough, they-- we went to Prague, little Esther, Max, and I. They gave us some very nice clothes. And they offered us more money. And I said, no. We don't need more money. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's-- through that album, we were able to come to America. I never sold it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first hear about the Nuremberg Trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- where we lived, in Sudeten.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had you heard about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard that they're-- they're making a big trial. Did you ever see that movie?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very powerful movie. And the cast in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had this album, and you heard about the Nuremberg Trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: So word got-- word got-- messages came through words, you know, when-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --we're talking about it. I never, never want to sell. And Klarsfeld decided to-- he went through all Germany. They didn't know where I am. You know, so he found out that this rabbi would remember me. And he lived in Cologne, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Farkas. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know the first name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that time in the '80s, he was still alive, and he was living in Cologne. And that's where Klarsfeld tracked him down.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide to go to Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I heard that a trial is-- and I want to see for myself. And it was-- the movie was done excellent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know you were going to testify?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was just something you wanted to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. For the ones who-- who can't be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: It [? was rough. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Max. Max and I. And he says, you're sure you want to go through? I says, yes. Just as I'm sure today that I want to be interviewed by you. You're a great help to me. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- thank you. When you arrived in Germany-- in-- in Nuremberg, rather, who did you speak to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To an American soldier there. But they had to translate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when you walked into the courtroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was sheer hell. And the man that I really could have killed with my two hands was there also on the-- on the stand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lucas, the one who separated me from my mother. Bernard Lucas. Till this day, I could kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was on trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else was on trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zaretsky, Bogar--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was at Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, this was in Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. In Nuremberg, who was on trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the-- the names of the actors, but I don't remember the names. Hess, Rudolf Hess. Goring.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days did you testify? How many days?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of questions did they ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just where were we imprisoned, for how long. But they-- they were Americans amongst them, you know. And like Richard Widmark, I mean, the-- the roles in that-- in that trial was-- I-- I didn't work for a whole month because Stanley Kramer came into Miami. And-- and somebody told him that if you need a witness, call her. She knows everything. And she's got still a mind that-- that works.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you testified for four days--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at the trial. And you were asked to testify what you witnessed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they use your album at the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My album. That's our album. It's everybody's album.

speaker: interviewer
text: They used the album. How did you feel after you finished testifying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Relieved.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you learn what the verdict was for these men?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned about it when I went home already, because I was very tired and worn out. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you when you learned that they were guilty?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they all said they're not guilty. Said it in German. Not-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: After you testified at Nuremberg, then you went back to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Sudeten, where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in the Sudeten before you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived there in '45. We got married there, Max and I. And we came to America November 3, I think, when President Truman was elected.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that you were called to testify at another trial. Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Frankfurt?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. In Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jerusalem. Yeah. It's not-- really not a place that I wanted to go. But I'm glad I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who contacted you about testifying at Eichmann's trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember-- some people from Israel. Simon Wiesenthal was a very frequent comer to America. And he once came into the Famous restaurant, and he asked for me. I never heard of him in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he sat down, and he saw the album. He says, how can you live with something like that? I says, you know, Mr. Wiesenthal, I couldn't live without it. Had you known Eichmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I saw him on a podium in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940-- '43 or-- '43, yeah. Because in '44, they took us away [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to go to Israel to testify at that trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was wonderful. Especially when I heard what's go-- what-- what they gonna do with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of questions did they ask you at the Eichmann trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I remember, do I speak German. I says, no, I didn't-- I wasn't born in Germany. So I don't have to speak German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how long you-- how many days you testified there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four days, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of the prosecutor or the judge?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did your husband go with you to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And to Frankfurt I went alone, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: He begged me, don't go. I says, no. I have to.

speaker: interviewer
text: You testified at the Eichmann trial in 1960--

speaker: interviewee
text: '60. '60, I think. '60. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you returned to the United States and were asked to testify a third time. Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Used to get calls. I used to get letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: From whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Germany. He told me his name was Dr.-- yeah, Dr. Hoffmayer. He was the judge of the trial. And they desperately need me to come there because I'm the only one who has proof to prove that what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, but I don't want to go. But you-- you better think about it. I said, what are you, warning me? I say, you don't need to warn me. And-- so one day, I'm just leaving the house, locking it up. And I heard the phone ring. So I went back. And he says to me, Mrs. Zelmanovic. I says, what can I do for you? He said, I want you to come to a trial. You're the one who has all the potent evidence. You want-- you don't want to help us out? I said, I don't have for strength to go through anymore what I went through. Well, he says, we'll try to spare you. But please come. They all say that the witnesses are crazy. They don't know what they're talking. They might be lying. And here you're walking around with something, and you don't want to come? So I called him nasty names. And I was really, you know, all worked up. And he asked to speak to my mother. I said, my mother is not alive, and you know that. How dare you hurt me after so many years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he started apologizing. Please forgive me. Please find room in your heart to forgive me. I didn't mean to, he says. But people are coming, you know, to testify, and you're refusing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I start breathing in a little air. I said, I'll tell you what. Why don't you make all the arrangements? I'm going to make myself a passport, and I'm coming. He says, you mean it? I says, yes, I'm coming. I called my husband at work. I [? say, ?] honey. I decided to go to Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, then go. He says-- because when you make up your mind, you do it. I says, I want to. And that's how I went to-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Frankfurt in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you to go back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was rough. It was really, really rough. I-- we-- the plane was arriving in Frankfurt, and the plane start-- started to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] land. And all of a sudden, the microphone came on. Mrs. Zelmanovic, please report to the information desk. And going over and over. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And got off the plane. Instead of going to the information desk, I went to a telephone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I called for the American embassy. Right away, the-- he came on. And I says, I'm an American citizen. I came to testify at the Auschwitz trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, could you be of any help to me? I'm a-- petrified being in Germany. That was the first time I am back to Germany. And so he says, please don't move. Just stay where you are. And I'm calling my chauffeur. We'll be there with the limousine. We'll pick you up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came with the limousine. And instead of going to the American embassy, he took us to a man named Max Bauer who was responsible for the Auschwitz trial in Frankfurt. As a matter of fact, he was here in the States a few weeks ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days did you testify at the Auschwitz trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, the questions that they asked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the defendants at that trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones I really remember is [? Hoffman. ?] He was a schoolteacher when Hitler came to power. And I forgot the name of the-- he was the photographer in Auschwitz. And I forgot who the other one was. I know his name, but not offhand.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the outcome of this trial? Were these men found guilty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really much. They put some in jail. It wasn't as powerful as the Nuremberg Trial. Nuremberg Trial was real powerful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think the Nuremberg Trials affected history?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to say because it was so soon after the war. And they weren't ready for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you satisfied with the verdicts that were reached at the Nuremberg Trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Eichmann trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Auschwitz trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I-- I was happy that I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your feelings today about the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saying I hate them, no. I don't hate them. Because they have to live with themselves. We took a tape with us when we went in '88. We were invited to go there for the 50th liberation of the Crystal Night. We took a-- a tape with us that was made in-- in Auschwitz-- in Miami. Because that crew came along with us to Auschwitz. And they made a documentary. It's unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I'd like to salvage some tapes that I have from there. It-- it-- it was a tape-- it was everything. But-- and-- and the Yad Vashem, I requested it before the-- the ceremony. I would like a cantor to be like, you know-- like it's a funeral. And it was right there in-- where the-- on the floors you got-- you were there in the Yad Vashem. I don't have to tell you. And it was really like a funeral.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your feelings today about people who deny that the Holocaust ever happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should it affect me that much that it-- it should hurt me? I can't hurt myself anymore. So I just stay away.

speaker: interviewer
text: This album was such a great part of your life. Where is it now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Yad Vashem-- safe place. When the book came out and we were in Germany, too, Eric and I kept going back through Israel. And I came in. And I said to Dr. Arad-- you probably heard of him. Dr. Arad-- he was the one in charge in those days. What a wonderful person. So when we came and I said to him, I brought a-- a-- the baby to his mother. And they were together in a glazed glass cage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to give it up now, that-- at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I realized that Eric was right. What-- he says, now, don't interrupt me. Wait till I'm finished. I-- I never waited till I-- he was finished. And I'm very glad that-- that's how it-- it was meant to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about having been the messenger and the keeper of that very, very important part of history?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that it was my destiny. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I kept it. Was never in hiding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was always there. If people call, no matter where they-- when I-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --somehow-- the-- it was revealed to the world that-- about me with the album. The next day, I had my house-- I had a big house in Miami. People from all walks of life, lawyers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people from-- from like Random House. From every-- you know. And I just didn't know-- and Eric was trying to call home, and the line is busy and busy, busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he finally called the neighbor across [? it. ?] He says, [? Helen. ?] Go and see if Lili's all right. I call, and there is no answer. And what I did is the same thing [LAUGHS] you said you-- we gonna do here. Take off the phone from the hook. We're gonna take a tape break now.
